conversation_starters.title: Canadian belugas faced ‘imminent risk’ of death before emergency flight to Chicago, marine park lawyer says
conversation_starters.summary: Unprecedented beluga whale rescue flight to Shedd Aquarium — quirky, high-profile local story. Work-safe opener. You could open with: 'Did you see they flew belugas into Chicago for the Shedd?'
conversation_starters.description: <p>The 30 beluga whales at Marineland Canada — including four now at Shedd Aquarium — were at “imminent risk” of being euthanized, a longtime advisor and lawyer for the shuttered aquatic park told the Chicago Sun-Times Wednesday.</p><p>“The bottom line is we have expended, since the beginning of COVID, all of our available reserves of funds to keep these animals alive and rehoused,” said Andrew Burns, speaking from Toronto, Canada.</p><p>Burns said no more whales are scheduled to leave Marineland this week, but that the goal is to remove all of them by Aug. The move is unprecedented — and controversial</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2018/2/14/18349072/drips-shark-bumps-mischievous-belugas-dancing-dolphins-40-years-at-the-shedd" >Drips, shark bumps, mischievous belugas, dancing dolphins: 40 years at the Shedd</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>“It’s going to be within weeks, but we’re being very cautious about telling the precise date because obviously for the safety of the animals, it’s best that the actual day of the move isn’t publicized in advance,” he said.</p><p>Burns also criticized Canadian animal rights advocates, saying their dreams of a future whale sanctuary off the coast of Nova Scotia are “worse than pie in the sky.”</p><p>“They have convinced large swaths of the public that that is the answer to whales in captivity and they have had opportunities for over a decade or more to put in place a functional facility … that could take whales, and they have failed unequivocally to do it,” Burns said, adding, “It’s a great idea, but it’s like saying we should go to Mars. … [Marineland] is not about fanciful ideas. What we’re about is trying to take care of these animals in a real situation right now.”</p><p>Some of those animal welfare advocates have told the Sun-Times that they have raised almost half of the animal law advocacy group.</p><p>“They should make up for this morally unconscionable business by funding sanctuaries, like the one currently under construction in Nova Scotia,” Labchuk added. “It's shameful that they have refused to devote a single penny of Marineland's massive wealth toward giving the whales what they deserve: a sanctuary."</p><p>Burns was effusive in his praise for Shedd and the other aquariums that have agreed to take the belugas. Burns said he was present “from start to finish” for the transport from Marineland Monday.</p><p>“To their absolute credit and our undying appreciation, they stepped up at the critical time,” Burns class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Shedd Aquarium and other aquariums that agreed to take the belugas “stepped up at the critical time,” said the lawyer for Marineland Canada. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Photo by ©Shedd Aquarium/Heidi Zeiger<br></p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Six belugas left Marineland Monday, four bound for Shedd and two more to SeaWorld in San Antonio. In preparation for the move, water was partially drained from one of the pools, The Canadian Press reported.</p><p>The belugas were placed into slings, before being hoisted into metal containers. They were then trucked to Toronto Pearson Airport, where they were flown in a Boeing 777 to Chicago and San Antonio. For the safety of the whales, pilots had to be careful not to take off or land too steeply, according to The Canadian Press.</p><p>Marineland in Niagara Falls, Ontario, has been closed to the public since 2024. Opened in 1961, the sprawling park has for years faced accusations of mistreating animals. The number of visitors had dwindled through the years.</p><p>Late last year, the financially strapped park applied for permits to ship the whales to a theme park in China, but Canadian authorities denied the permits, determining the move wasn’t in the best interests of the whales. Marineland then told the Canadian government that the park had two options for the whales: relocation or euthanasia.</p><p>Burns said Wednesday that it costs about $1.5 million Canadian a month to care for the whales. That equates to about $1 million in U.S. currency.</p><p>Euthanasia was a “real, direct and imminent risk,” he said.</p><p>Some Canadian animal welfare advocates have described Marineland’s threat to euthanize the whales as a “manufactured crisis,” that essentially forced the Canadian government to approve sending the whales to four American and one Spanish aquarium.</p><p>The animal welfare advocates say they fear that the aquariums will now breed the Marineland whales, leading to more marine mammals living out their lives in captivity.</p><p>Burns said there are no birth control methods for beluga whales. “Animal welfare activists have very fixed ideological positions that do not relate in any way to the actual needs and experiences of animals,” Burns said. “So in demanding that they not breed, what they are doing is saying that these animals cannot be allowed to live in their normal social groupings." </p><p>He continued: “What they are really saying about Marineland is: Take family groups that have been together for decades and rip them apart and separate them so that families are broken apart. … I don’t agree with that at all."</p>

href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wutklhy-l-u/" target="_blank" >Alma Campos</a></h5><p><b>Accountability push</b>: Mexico's government has begun taking legal action to seek accountability for the deaths of 17 of its citizens during U.S.Immigration and Customs Enforcement operations — 14 of them in ICE custody and three during other "arrest operations." Mexico President Claudia Sheinbaum has referred to these deaths as "homicides."</p><p><b>Midway Blitz tie</b>: Among those for whom Mexico is seeking accountability is Silverio Villegas González, who was shot and killed by an ICE agent during a traffic stop in suburban Franklin Park in September. Villegas González had lived in the United States since 2007and was the father of two young children.</p><p><b>Legal action</b>: Mexico officials have filed criminal complaints with state prosecutors in Illinois and elsewhere and plan to submit a complaint to the U.S. Department of Justice, seeking independent investigations into the deaths. The Mexican government also issued cease-and-desist letters directing U.S. detention centers to preserve records and called for a formal review by the United Nations Human Rights Council.</p><p><b>What will this do?</b>: Legal experts and immigration advocates say it's unclear whether U.S. authorities will cooperate with an investigation, but the process still could put political pressure on the United States to take action and potentially produce evidence that could be used in civil lawsuits. Mexico can’t compel the U.S. to investigate or collect evidence, experts say.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wutklhy-l-o/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>HEALTHCARE 🩺</h4><div class="Enhancement" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wutklhy-l-p/" target="_blank" >Elvia Malagón</a></h5><p><b>New study</b>: New research from Northwestern Medicine shows for the first time that women suffer more severe neurologic symptoms due to long COVID, such as brain fog and numbness, than men. The research, published Tuesday in the Annals of Clinical and Translational Neurology, also found women with long COVID experienced worse effects on their quality of life and cognitive functioning than men.</p><p><b>Common symptoms</b>: The research is based on evaluations of 2,329 patients who sought care at Northwestern’s COVID clinic from May 2020 through August 2025. Women reported experiencing neurologic symptoms for about 16 months on average, according to the journal article. Some of the most common neurologic symptoms are brain fog, numbness, dizziness, tinnitus, blurred vision and muscle pain.</p><p><a class="Link" class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wutklhy-l-td/" target="_blank" >Food brings this family together and one recipe reminds them of home</a></h3><p></p><h5>Reporting by <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wutklhy-l-th/" target="_blank" >Araceli Gómez-Aldana</a></h5><p>Food has been at the center of Teddy and Alexis Vejar’s lives since they were kids. They remember walking around their neighborhood in Morelia, Michoacán, where people would serve dinner out of their homes.</p><p>Today, the brothers own Jarabe in Little Italy. The restaurant opened in 2015 and prides itself on authentic Mexican cuisine. When they began developing their recipes, they focused on making things from scratch, like their grandmother did growing up. </p><p>Of all the dishes they grew up eating, one remains their favorite all these years later: Cochinita pibil. They serve it at their restaurant.</p><p>Teddy and Alexis have fond memories of living in Ukrainian Village when their dad began making homemade cochinita pibil for them and their extended family. Even today, the family gets together to eat the dish.</p><p>Cochinita pibil is a pulled pork dish. The pork is marinated in achiote paste and served with tortillas and pickled red onions. It’s considered one of Yucatán’s traditional dishes, and it’s a favorite celebration meal for the Vejar family.</p><p>WBEZ reporter Araceli Gómez-Aldana and photojournalist Manuel Martinez met up with the Vejars to learn more about what the dish means to them.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wutklhy-l-tk/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>WATCH: MAKING COCHINITA PIBIL ▶️</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/OazrsIWZC3Q?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Tastes from Home: Tortillas, togetherness and time well spent."></iframe> </div> </div></div><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p><i>What Chicago restaurant feels like home to you? Tell us why.</i></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Reply to this question via email</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</i></p><p>In Wednesday's newsletter, we asked you: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wutklhy-l-il/" target="_blank" >What longtime Chicago institution should more people know about?</a></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><p>"People should know more about the Chicago Pedway.  It's quite interesting, and handy in the winter." — <i>Christine Bock</i></p><p>"The Driehaus Museum is certainly one of the best hidden gems of Chicago institutions, now offering pay-what-you-wish Wednesday evenings and free music programming every third Wednesday in their Murphy Auditorium." — <i>Katy O’Malley</i></p><p>"Chicago Cultural Center. It's a free museum essentially. They had a super dope Chicago House exhibit a few years ago." — <i>Robbie Jason Ferraren</i></p><p>"The Museum of Broadcast Communications. Not very well known ... Even those who know of it may not be aware that it’s back in business after a long hiatus." — <i>David Fell</i></p><p>"Ed Paschke Art Center in Jefferson Park; Wrightwood 659 gallery, in a four story structure designed by Tadao Ando in Lincoln Park; and The Arts Club of Chicago in Streeterville." — <i>Victoria Cunha</i></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated b</b>y: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you.
